description: Close or join a tear, split, or modest break in an object or material so it can be used again. 'whori' is the ordinary mending verb for garments, nets, baskets, and similar things; 'shiroka' (repair) centres technical recovery of function.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for unvoiced 'wh', letting breath pass into stressed middle-height back 'o'. Make a tap or short trill at the alveolar ridge for 'r' and finish with high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: A hushed rounded opening gives way to one small voiced tap. The compact movement suits a modest repair made close to the hands.
usage_notes: The damaged object or material is the direct object. Tool and method take their ordinary phrases. Relationships use their own verbs, such as 'warosha' (reconcile), unless a speaker deliberately introduces a mending image.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Mending extends the use of an object and the materials already committed to it. The small repair can be enough; replacement is not the automatic answer.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Cloth, nets, and baskets invite different hands and tools, but all teach the same habit of noticing a small failure while it can still be mended.

semantic_domains.activity: hands-on closure of a tear, split, or modest break
semantic_domains.creation: material work that returns an object to use
semantic_domains.physical: joining damaged material at the failed place
